author: user
subject: directive: collapse EW/EOM and resume logic into durable-boundary state machine
summary: Architectural priority for the Codex Layer 3c implementer: the reviewer diagnosis is sharper than simply saying the EW/EOM matrix is exhaustive. The current shape is phase x signal with each cell hand-specified, which is the classic unrolled form of a state machine. Keep the exhaustive EW/EOM table in the design/spec as the normative authority; do not simplify the spec by deleting cases. The implementation should move toward a small, well-named durable-boundary or commit-point state machine that tracks the last durably committed tape file and the outstanding not-yet-durable commitments. EW handling should be expressed as one reserve-check predicate: does remaining reserve cover every not-yet-durable commitment. EOM handling should bypass reserve as moot and roll back to the last durable commit point. The phases should differ mainly by what commitments are outstanding at that moment: object block, filemark, sidecar in a cluster, final bootstrap, etc. This same durable-boundary abstraction should also inform crash/resume/reposition logic, because resume is the backward-looking version of the same question: what was the last durable boundary and how do we return there cleanly. Treat this as an architecture cleanup/refactor priority after any open High/Critical Claude findings and alongside removal of legacy inline parity. The goal is to reduce sink.rs/recovery.rs density without weakening the v0.4.4 normative semantics or the existing EW/EOM coverage.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 1342c48e (introduce ParitySink DurableBoundaryState as the first bounded step of user directive 53a10827's durable-boundary state-machine refactor). Finding [no finding]: this is a clean, conservative first step that introduces the right abstraction without changing observable semantics. The DurableBoundaryState type at sink.rs:65-167 captures the directive's load-bearing concept in two fields: last_committed_tape_file_number (the most recent durably-committed boundary, Option<u32>) and outstanding (at most ONE in-flight tape file via OutstandingTapeFileCommit { kind, tape_file_number, started_after_tape_file_number }). The three primitive operations are well-named and tight: (a) begin_tape_file(kind, n) at sink.rs:86-116 enforces two invariants — no nested outstanding (sink.rs:91-95: 'cannot start a tape file while another tape file is not durable'), and contiguous numbering (sink.rs:96-104: n == last_committed+1 or n==0 if no prior commit, with an explicit overflow guard); (b) commit_tape_file(kind, n) at sink.rs:118-127 advances last_committed and clears outstanding only after expect_outstanding matches; (c) abandon_tape_file(kind, n) at sink.rs:129-138 RESTORES last_committed to the started_after_tape_file_number value — this is the directive's 'roll back to the last durable commit point' semantics, NOT just clearing outstanding. The started_after field at sink.rs:113 captures the pre-begin boundary at begin time, so abandon is symmetric with begin. The expect_outstanding helper at sink.rs:150-166 is a sharp guard with two error paths: 'no outstanding tape file' and 'outstanding tape file does not match'. Production wiring (verified at four call sites): (1) ParitySink field declaration at sink.rs:437; (2) resume-seeded construction at sink.rs:522-523 calls DurableBoundaryState::from_committed_prefix(resume_seed.committed_prefix) which seeds last_committed to the last entry in the catalog's committed prefix; (3) fresh construction at sink.rs:568 uses DurableBoundaryState::new() (boundary at None); (4) wrappers commit_tape_file_boundary/abandon_tape_file_boundary_or/abandon_active_object_boundary at sink.rs:1496-1526 give clean call sites at object begin (sink.rs:1486-1487), bootstrap begin (sink.rs:924-925) and on error paths. The abandon_tape_file_boundary_or pattern at sink.rs:1505-1518 preserves the original error if abandon succeeds (and surfaces the boundary error otherwise) — this is the right ordering: caller's domain error wins, boundary-tracking integrity is secondary diagnostic. Two new unit tests pin the boundary semantics: (a) durable_boundary_abandons_failed_file_to_last_committed_tape_file at sink.rs:1786 verifies the success-then-rollback-then-RETRY-at-same-tape-file-number-then-commit cycle — the retry-at-same-number assertion at sink.rs:1817-1819 is load-bearing because it proves abandon truly reverts last_committed (if it merely cleared outstanding, the retry would skip to tape_file=2 and the test would fail); (b) durable_boundary_rejects_nested_or_gap_commitments at sink.rs:1827 exercises four distinct error paths — first-file-must-be-0 (sink.rs:1830-1835), no-nested-outstanding (sink.rs:1840-1845), commit-kind-mismatch (sink.rs:1847-1852), and no-skipping-gaps (sink.rs:1857-1862). Crucially, the existing §7.5.1 EW/EOM matrix (287 unit tests, +2 from 285) and the §7.7 commit-durability barrier behavior are preserved — this pass introduces the abstraction without collapsing the call sites that produce the normative behavior. The directive's three deliverables map cleanly: 'last durably committed tape file' = last_committed_tape_file_number; 'outstanding not-yet-durable commitments' = outstanding (singleton, kind-tagged); EOM rollback = abandon_tape_file. The EW reserve-check predicate consolidation and the resume-module integration are explicitly NOT in this pass — codex correctly scoped to writer-side bookkeeping first. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 287 passed, +2 from 285; library 214; scsi 95; cli 16; scan_round_trip 14; doc-tests/ignored as expected). Residual risk: the directive's other half — collapsing the EW reserve-check into one predicate ('does remaining reserve cover every not-yet-durable commitment') — is NOT in this pass; the per-cell EW handling at sink.rs:1481-1486 (sidecar body EOM), sink.rs:1496-1501 (sidecar trailing filemark EOM), sink.rs:1650-1655 (object data EOM), sink.rs:786-790 (object trailing filemark EOM) still each have their own end_of_medium check. A unified reserve predicate would consolidate these four gates plus the recovery's started_after rollback into one decision point — that's the natural next bounded step. The resume module (crates/remanence-parity/src/resume.rs) and recovery module (crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs) are not yet routed through DurableBoundaryState; resume currently uses its own AppendPlan / append_after_tape_file_number tracking. Aligning resume's append-plan calculation with from_committed_prefix's last-entry semantics is the right next consolidation. The TapeFileKind enum's three variants (Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap) align with the three known commitment kinds — but if a future v0.5+ design introduces a fourth kind (e.g. an in-tape catalog snapshot), the kind-tagging here would need extension; the current PartialEq match at sink.rs:160 is closed-world. The boundary helper is private (sink.rs:65 has no `pub`); if future cross-module callers (resume.rs, recovery.rs) need it, the visibility will need lifting. No regression in the 285→287 test count and clean clippy/fmt confirm the integration is non-disruptive.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 1dd6eb5a (unify writer EW reserve into one runtime predicate per user directive 53a10827's remaining half). Finding [no finding]: this is exactly the EW reserve-check consolidation I named in 02095ed5's residual risk, executed cleanly. Five coordinated pieces: (1) Event enum at sink.rs:42-50 names the six EW-relevant write events (ObjectDataBlock, ObjectFilemark, SidecarBlock, SidecarFilemark, BootstrapBlock, BootstrapFilemark) — covering every tape-file kind × {body, trailing filemark} cell that can carry an EW. (2) Reserve-cost-blocks function at sink.rs:52-63 maps each event to its reserve consumption in blocks: ObjectDataBlock is 0 (object body writes are tracked separately via object_blocks_written), other events charge input.{object,sidecar,bootstrap}_filemark_blocks (filemark events) or 1 (sidecar/bootstrap body blocks). The asymmetry between ObjectDataBlock=0 and SidecarBlock=1/BootstrapBlock=1 is deliberate: object body blocks are part of the projected_object_blocks budget (counted via remaining_projected_object_blocks at sink.rs:109-115), whereas sidecar and bootstrap body blocks are pure reserve consumption. (3) State at sink.rs:65-71 tracks two counters: object_blocks_written and reserve_blocks_consumed. (4) The single predicate ensure_covers_outstanding_commitments() at sink.rs:108-154 is the directive's load-bearing 'does remaining reserve cover every not-yet-durable commitment' check: it computes remaining_blocks (input.remaining_tape_blocks - consumed_blocks) and outstanding_commitment_blocks (remaining_projected_object_blocks + remaining_reserve_blocks) and returns ParityError::CapacityReserveExceeded { cause: TapeCapacity, ... } if remaining < outstanding. The checked_sub at sink.rs:131-142 returns CapacityReserveExceeded with remaining_blocks=Some(0) and reserve_blocks=Some(remaining_reserve_blocks) — sharper diagnostics than the prior ad hoc per-cell gates. (5) The single dispatch point record_success_and_check_early_warning_reserve at sink.rs:1563-1580 has the load-bearing structural invariant: line 1569 returns early when end_of_medium is true ('EOM paths bypass this reserve predicate' — directly per the directive); line 1576-1578 invokes ensure_covers_outstanding_commitments() ONLY when early_warning is true. Six production wiring points exercise this dispatch: ObjectFilemark at sink.rs:853-854, BootstrapBlock at sink.rs:1031-1032, BootstrapFilemark at sink.rs:1065-1066, SidecarBlock at sink.rs:1413-1414, SidecarFilemark at sink.rs:1448-1449, ObjectDataBlock at sink.rs:1699-1700 — replacing what were previously six scattered EW gates. Reserve state installed at sink.rs:1554 inside begin_object_with_capacity_reserve from the validated CapacityReserveInput/CapacityReserveReport pair — so the seed values come from the same capacity-reserve admission path the v0.4.4 design § 7.5 specifies. The new test sink.rs:3812 object_data_early_warning_uses_single_runtime_reserve_predicate exercises the consolidated path with a deliberate exhaustion: it directly mutates sink.early_warning_reserve.input.remaining_tape_blocks to 0 mid-stream (sink.rs:3820-3824), writes a block that the raw fixture flags as EW (vec![1] = EW on block 1), asserts the production returns TapeIoError::OperationFailed with both 'required reserve' and 'TapeCapacity' in the message (sink.rs:3829-3835), and asserts finish_object returns Invariant('poisoned') (sink.rs:3837-3845). Crucially, raw.ew_blocks_seen == [1] (the EW landed) and raw.filemark_count == 0 (NO object filemark or map entry was written) — proving the predicate fires BEFORE the §7.7 commit-durability barrier. The §7.5.1 EW/EOM matrix (now exercised through one predicate instead of six gates) and the §7.7 commit-durability ordering are preserved — the regression net is the existing 287 EW-related tests in remanence-parity (now 290 with this run's +1, the durable-boundary tests count from ca4d3a5d, and the seed-from-resume test). Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 290 passed, +1 from 289; library 214; scsi 95; cli 16; scan_round_trip 14; doc-tests/ignored as expected). Residual risk: the new test exhausts reserve via direct field mutation (sink.early_warning_reserve.input.remaining_tape_blocks = 0) rather than via realistic per-block consumption. A 'natural exhaustion' test — long object that fills the reserve over many real blocks, then EW fires on a late block and the predicate detects the running shortfall — would prove the running-sum bookkeeping in record_successful_event at sink.rs:83-106 is correct beyond what the mutation test exercises. Sidecar-spool-reserve exhaustion (CapacityReserveCause::ParitySpoolCapacity) is not exercised here — the test uses TapeCapacity only. The predicate's three checked_add/checked_sub overflow guards (sink.rs:112, 122, 127, 134) are not separately exercised for the wraparound case; that's low value but unverified. The earlier per-cell EW gates that the consolidation replaces — if any of them carried EW-specific business rules NOT captured by the single predicate's remaining<outstanding inequality — could regress silently; the 287→290 test delta is small enough that this should have been caught, but a deliberate code-archaeology sweep comparing each old per-cell gate against the new predicate-only invariant would close that residual definitively. The user directive 53a10827's third deliverable (recovery.rs read-side and reposition/resume integration with the same durable-boundary concept) is still not addressed — recovery.rs uses scoped_map.scope.recoverable for its watermark check (recovery.rs:103) which is the read-side equivalent of the writer's reserve predicate, but is not yet expressed in terms of the same DurableBoundaryState abstraction. That's the next natural bounded step.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 800eb231 (resume EW + second-sidecar catalog-commit-failure gate — closes the two residual cells I named in 02095ed5 and ca4d3a5d). Finding [no finding]: this is exactly the asymmetric residual I named in 02095ed5: 'Multi-sidecar resume with commit-callback-fail on sidecar 2 (after sidecar 1 succeeds) is also not exercised' AND 'EW-combined with commit-callback failure'. The new test emit_resume_rebuilt_sidecars_abandons_second_boundary_when_ew_catalog_commit_fails at resume.rs:1544 combines both into one fixture: (a) 2 rebuilt sidecars from an 8-ordinal committed prefix, (b) EW on the SECOND sidecar's first body block (resume.rs:1570 with_early_warning_on_block(second_sidecar_first_block)) AND EW on the SECOND sidecar's trailing filemark (resume.rs:1571 with_early_warning_on_filemark_index(1)), (c) first sidecar's callback returns Ok, (d) second sidecar's callback returns Err(ParityError::Invariant('catalog transaction failed for second resume sidecar')) (resume.rs:1584-1590). The error wrapping is rigorous — the ResumeAppend message must contain three substrings at resume.rs:1595-1611: (i) 'resume sidecar {second_tape_file_number}' (proving the error names the failing sidecar), (ii) 'catalog commit failed after filemark barrier' (proving §7.7's commit-durability barrier semantics — the error fired AFTER the barrier, not before), (iii) 'catalog transaction failed for second resume sidecar' (proving the inner callback error is preserved through the wrap). The ew_blocks_seen==[second_sidecar_first_block] and ew_filemarks_seen==1 assertions at resume.rs:1612-1613 prove the two EW signals landed precisely where the fixture targeted — not on the first sidecar. The event stream at resume.rs:1615-1638 pins the canonical 2-sidecar barrier sequence: Position(append) → first sidecar body → WriteFilemark → Position(first_barrier) → Commit(first, OK) → Position(first_barrier) → second sidecar body → WriteFilemark → Position(second_barrier) → Commit(second, the failing one) → STOP. Critically, the FIRST sidecar's Commit event is present in the expected stream — proving sidecar 1 DID commit cleanly through the same code path that subsequently aborts on sidecar 2. And the SECOND sidecar's Commit event is also present — proving the callback fired AFTER the §7.7 barrier (the position+filemark+position-after sequence) but the production catches the Err and surfaces it. No further events follow the second Commit, proving the abort is immediate; the durable_boundary.abandon_tape_file path at resume.rs:328-336 (which I reviewed in ca4d3a5d) is the load-bearing abandon mechanism here, and the event stream's silence after the second Commit is the externally-observable evidence that the boundary rollback worked. Combined with ca4d3a5d's no-EW commit-failure test (resume.rs:1490), the resume crash-window matrix now covers: {no EW, EW on body, EW on filemark, EW on body+filemark} × {commit success, commit failure} on the second-sidecar position. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 291 passed, +1 from 290; library 214; scsi 95; cli 16; scan_round_trip 14; doc-tests/ignored as expected). Residual risk: A symmetric case — commit failure on sidecar 1 BEFORE sidecar 2 attempts — is not separately exercised; the existing emit_resume_rebuilt_sidecars_abandons_boundary_when_catalog_commit_fails at resume.rs:1490 already covers 'commit-fail on sidecar 1 in a single-sidecar rebuild', but the explicit '2 rebuilt, sidecar 1 fails, sidecar 2 never attempted' interleaving is not exercised — the loop ordering at resume.rs:289-341 would short-circuit on the first Err so this case should be safe by construction, but a regression test would confirm. The recovery.rs durable-boundary integration that I named in bce3b685 (recovery.rs:103's scoped_map.scope.recoverable as the read-side equivalent) is still not addressed — that remains the natural next bounded step for directive 53a10827. The natural-exhaustion EW reserve test (bce3b685 residual) is also still open. Three-or-more sidecar resume topologies with commit-fail on the 3rd+ sidecar are not exercised because the rebuild fixture geometry caps at 2 sidecars; a fixture with a larger committed prefix would unlock that cell.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 77ec76c1 (natural EW reserve exhaustion regression — closes bce3b685/a66dda4d's natural-exhaustion residual). Finding [no finding]: this is exactly the realistic 'tape fills up via real writes, then EW fires on a late block and the predicate detects the running shortfall' test I named. The fixture at sink.rs:3856 is sharp: (a) construct a real 12-block object plus its actual parity sidecar via encode_sidecar_tape_file at sink.rs:3859-3876 (not a mock — uses the production codec); (b) build a reserve_input that is admit-valid but deliberately under-modeled by ONE block: sidecar_index_block_count = shard_index_block_count - 1 (sink.rs:3886-3887). This is the smallest possible under-modeling that admits the object at begin_object but trips during runtime sidecar emission. The other budget knobs are stripped to zero slack: sidecar_filemark_blocks=0, remaining_bootstrap_count=0, safety_margin_blocks=0 (sink.rs:3888-3890). remaining_tape_blocks is then set to exactly the admission's required_tape_blocks via input.evaluate() at sink.rs:3891-3894 — proving the start IS valid before any natural consumption. (c) EW lands on the LAST sidecar body block: ew_on_last_sidecar_body_block = object_blocks.len() + sidecar_block_count at sink.rs:3882. The raw fixture vec![ew_on_last_sidecar_body_block] at sink.rs:3896 means EW fires only on block index 12 + sidecar_block_count, after all 12 object blocks and all but the final sidecar block have written clean. The test does NOT touch sink.early_warning_reserve directly — natural consumption alone drives the predicate to the shortfall point. Assertions: (i) finish_object returns ParityError::CapacityReserveExceeded with the exact field shape at sink.rs:3910-3927 — cause==TapeCapacity, projected_object_blocks==12, remaining_blocks==Some(0), reserve_blocks==Some(0), no spool fields — confirming the saturating_sub branch at sink.rs:131-142 fires (the predicate exhausted remaining_tape_blocks naturally); (ii) filemark_map contains ONLY the object entry at sink.rs:3934-3938 — the object filemark wrote before sidecar emission so the object IS committed (this is the same unprotected-object catalog state I reviewed in 4e6b4af2/0ea10ddc/9045b889, but here arrived at via reserve exhaustion rather than EOM, demonstrating the two paths produce the same catalog state); (iii) poisoned retry at sink.rs:3940-3948; (iv) raw.ew_blocks_seen pinned exactly to the targeted block at sink.rs:3951; (v) raw.filemark_count == 1 (only the object filemark) and raw.block_count == ew_on_last_sidecar_body_block (sink.rs:3952-3956) — proving sidecar emission halted at the EW block and the sidecar trailing filemark was NEVER written. Combined with bce3b685's direct-mutation test (object_data_early_warning_uses_single_runtime_reserve_predicate at sink.rs:3812), the EW reserve predicate is now exercised through two complementary paths: synthetic mutation (proving the predicate fires when remaining_blocks==0) AND natural consumption (proving the running-sum bookkeeping in record_successful_event at sink.rs:83-106 correctly tracks 12 object data writes + N-1 sidecar block writes + 1 EW-bearing sidecar block to reach the same shortfall). The natural-exhaustion path also exercises the §7.7 ordering: object filemark commits BEFORE the sidecar body even starts, so the object IS catalog-visible at the moment of failure — and the predicate correctly identifies the failed sidecar as ParitySpoolCapacity-or-TapeCapacity (TapeCapacity in this case, because the under-modeling was on sidecar_index_block_count which costs tape blocks). Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (remanence-parity 292 passed, +1 from 291; library 214; scsi 95; cli 16; scan_round_trip 14; doc-tests/ignored as expected). Residual risk: The under-modeling-by-1 approach is clever but it's a single point in the parameter space; under-modeling by 2+ blocks, or under-modeling sidecar_filemark_blocks specifically (rather than sidecar_index_block_count), or under-modeling remaining_bootstrap_count, are not separately exercised. ParitySpoolCapacity cause path is still not exercised — only TapeCapacity is. The recovery.rs durable-boundary integration that I named in bce3b685 (recovery.rs:103's scoped_map.scope.recoverable as the read-side equivalent of the writer's reserve predicate) remains the next bounded step. The interaction between the EW reserve predicate and the §7.5.1 EOM path on the same sidecar (e.g. reserve-exhausted-AND-EOM-on-same-block) is not exercised — the production code at sink.rs:1569 returns early when end_of_medium is true, BYPASSING the reserve predicate; this is by design (the directive 53a10827 says 'EOM paths bypass reserve as moot and roll back to the last durable commit point') but a regression test that exercises both signals on one write would prove the precedence is correct.

This proves the EOM gate at sink.rs:1481-1486 (the explicit `if outcome.end_of_medium` check inside the sidecar body loop) wins over the runtime reserve predicate, exactly matching the production code's structural ordering: write_block → inspect end_of_medium first (returns Invariant if true) → only THEN call record_success_and_check_early_warning_reserve. The reserve predicate's own bypass at sink.rs:1569 ('if end_of_medium { return Ok(()); }') is the second line of defense — but in this test the inner sidecar body writer never even reaches that point because the explicit EOM check at sink.rs:1481-1486 returns first. summary: Review of codex entry 477c08a7 (recovery durable-prefix peer erasure regression + Some(0) prefix coverage). Finding [no finding]: this closes the two residuals I named in b38f2fa7 in one bounded pass. (1) Peer-erasure exhaustion test at recovery.rs:3055 — the fixture I named in 1c0466c4 and b38f2fa7 ('enough peers ARE beyond the boundary that the recovery exhausts its m-erasure budget'). The geometry is tight: scheme(3,1,1) gives k=3 data + m=1 parity per stripe, with the m-erasure budget exactly 1. The catalog has 1 committed object block + 2 suffix object blocks (forming one full 3-data-shard stripe), bootstrap(0,1), object(1,1,0), parity_sidecar(2, ...), object(3, 2, 1). validated_prefix_tape_files=Some(3) at recovery.rs:3076 admits tape files 0, 1, 2 (bootstrap + first object + sidecar) into the durable boundary; tape file 3 (the second object containing the 2 suffix peer ordinals) is unvalidated. Crucially MapScope::Prefix uses map_total_data_ordinals=1 (only the first object's ordinal is in the validated data prefix) but highest_protected_ordinal=3 (the sidecar's watermark claims all 3 ordinals are protected). scope.recoverable(0) passes (both checks succeed), failed_position resolves to tape_file=1 (inside boundary), sidecar_entry at tape_file=2 (inside boundary) — so the two ensure_inside_durable_recovery_boundary checks at recovery.rs:125 and recovery.rs:147 both pass. Recovery configures the SCSI source, loads the sidecar, and starts peer reads. The peer-read function at recovery.rs:454-477 now checks contains_committed_tape_file for each peer position: peers at ordinals 1 and 2 resolve to tape_file=3 which is > last_committed=2, so the peer-read returns Ok(None) (treated as erasure) BEFORE attempting tape I/O. With m=1 and 3 erasures (failed shard + 2 filtered peers), RS recovery returns ParityError::Unrecoverable. The assertions at recovery.rs:3093-3109 are sharp: stripe == ordinal_to_stripe(0), lost_count == 3, limit == scheme.parity_blocks_per_stripe (==1). The post-I/O invariant at recovery.rs:3110 confirms configure_fixed_block_size DID happen (Some(BLOCK_SIZE)) — contrasting with the pre-I/O bail cases (cf24d3e0 and 1c0466c4's recovery_rejects_sidecar_beyond_durable_prefix_before_tape_io). The peer-LBA filter assertion at recovery.rs:3111-3116 iterates suffix_peer_lbas (object_lbas_for_ordinals for ordinals 1 and 2 in tape_file=3) and asserts NONE of them appear in raw.read_lbas — proving the peer filter prevents tape reads for unvalidated peer positions, not just discards their results. This is the strongest form of the defense-in-depth property: unvalidated peer bytes never touch the SCSI bus, never reach the CRC check, never count as trusted shards — only against the m-erasure budget. (2) Some(0) extension at durable.rs:321-332 — extends durable_boundary_seeds_read_side_from_scoped_prefix with an additional ScopedFilemarkMap whose validated_prefix_tape_files=Some(0), and asserts from_scoped_map returns Ok with last_committed_tape_file_number==None, plus contains_committed_tape_file(0)==false. This is the fail-closed semantic: zero validated tape files means no tape file is trusted, NOT 'tape file 0 is trusted by default'. Three cases now exercised in one test: complete map (None → last entry), Some(n) prefix (entries[n-1]), Some(0) (None / empty boundary). Recommended fix: none. summary: Review of codex entry 98f40be4 (durable-prefix recovery parity-peer accounting regression — closes the parity-peer-budget residual from d182846b and 1c0466c4). Finding [no finding]: this test combines filtered data peer + damaged parity peer in one recovery, proving they BOTH count against the m-erasure budget. Setup at recovery.rs:3193: scheme(3,2,1) with k=3, m=2 (2 parity per stripe); 2 committed object blocks + 1 suffix object block = one full 3-data-shard stripe; validated_prefix=Some(3) admits bootstrap + first object + sidecar but excludes the suffix at tape_file=3. Sidecar has 2 parity shards. parity0_lba is marked unreadable to simulate damage. Recovery target ordinal 0: (a) scope.recoverable(0) passes; (b) boundary gates pass (failed at tape_file=1, sidecar at tape_file=2, both in last_committed=2); (c) peer reads — data peer ordinal 1 (tape_file=1, in boundary) → real read; data peer ordinal 2 (tape_file=3, out of boundary) → filter returns None, erasure; parity peer 0 (in boundary, but unreadable) → read attempt fails → erasure; parity peer 1 (in boundary, intact) → real read; (d) 3 erasures (failed data + filtered data + damaged parity) > m=2 → Unrecoverable. The four LBA assertions at recovery.rs:3256-3271 are the load-bearing test: committed_peer_lba IS in read_lbas (in-boundary data peer was read); suffix_peer_lba NOT in read_lbas (out-of-boundary data peer filtered BEFORE tape I/O); parity0_lba IS in read_lbas (the DAMAGED in-boundary parity peer was ATTEMPTED — proving the parity-peer path goes through tape I/O and is NOT pre-filtered or pre-skipped); parity1_lba IS in read_lbas (the intact in-boundary parity peer was read normally). The lost_count==3 assertion at recovery.rs:3248-3251 is the unified-budget invariant: data erasures and parity erasures contribute equally to the m-budget. Architectural insight: this test reveals that parity-peer filtering as a distinct concept is subsumed by the sidecar-level boundary check at recovery.rs:147 — sidecar parity peers are colocated with the sidecar's own tape_file_number, so if the sidecar is in-boundary, its parity peers are too, and if the sidecar is out-of-boundary, the recovery is rejected pre-I/O at recovery.rs:147 before any parity peer is touched. So 'parity-peer filtering' in the strict sense of 'per-peer boundary check applied to parity peers individually' is structurally unreachable in v0.4.4 — the boundary structure makes parity-peer-individual filtering equivalent to whole-sidecar filtering. Data peers, in contrast, live in different object tape files than the sidecar, so per-peer data filtering IS meaningful and tested separately. This is a subtle but important architectural property: the per-tape-file boundary check is sufficient because each tape file has uniform parity-status semantics (an object tape file has only data peers, a sidecar tape file has only parity peers + metadata). Test count: 305→306 (+1). Recommended fix: none. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry d88abd59 (runtime sidecar-filemark reserve under-modeling — closes the natural sidecar_filemark_blocks under-modeling residual from 7aa8630c). Finding [no finding]: this is a production behavior change (not just a test). Production change at sink.rs:67-71 — new helper filemark_reserve_cost_blocks(model_blocks: u64) -> u64 returns `model_blocks.max(1)`. The three filemark events in reserve_cost_blocks() at sink.rs:56/58/60-62 (ObjectFilemark, SidecarFilemark, BootstrapFilemark) now route through this helper, charging AT LEAST 1 block against the runtime reserve for every successful filemark, even when the admission-time estimate is 0. This is a defensive change: previously a catalog could declare sidecar_filemark_blocks=0 and the runtime predicate would charge nothing for filemark writes, allowing real consumption to go uncharged and the predicate to miss the under-modeling. With the clamp, even an explicit 0 declaration is treated as 1 at the runtime predicate, while admission-time evaluate() at capacity.rs is unchanged (still uses the raw 0). This introduces an intentional asymmetry: admission with sidecar_filemark_blocks=0 + remaining_tape_blocks=exact-fit will now ALWAYS trip the predicate on the first filemark write — effectively making 'admit with 0 filemark estimate' a runtime poison pill. This is correct defensive behavior because filemarks ALWAYS take at least 1 block on tape; a catalog that declares 0 is buggy by definition. The clamp preserves nonzero values: if model_blocks==N>=1, returns N unchanged — so correctly-modeled estimates are not penalized. New test at sink.rs:4405 sidecar_filemark_early_warning_detects_under_modeled_filemark_reserve directly exercises this production change: a 12-block object + full encoded sidecar with sidecar_index_block_count and parity_shards_per_epoch correctly modeled BUT sidecar_filemark_blocks=0 (the deliberate under-model). safety_margin=0, remaining_bootstrap_count=0, remaining_tape_blocks=required at admission. EW lands on the sidecar trailing filemark (filemark 2, the second filemark in the write sequence; first filemark = object trailing, second = sidecar trailing). Flow: object data writes clean → object filemark writes clean → record_successful_event(ObjectFilemark) charges filemark_reserve_cost_blocks(input.object_filemark_blocks) which clamps to max(1) → sidecar body writes clean → sidecar trailing filemark writes with EW → record_successful_event(SidecarFilemark) charges max(0, 1) = 1 block. Now the cumulative consumed_blocks exceeds the admission budget by the +1 charges from each filemark. The predicate's ensure_covers_outstanding_commitments() returns CapacityReserveExceeded{cause:TapeCapacity, remaining=Some(0), reserve=Some(0)} (sink.rs:4454-4473). Downstream: filemark_map contains ONLY the object entry (sink.rs:4475-4484) — sidecar push_parity_sidecar was blocked by the predicate firing BEFORE the §7.7 commit barrier. finish_object on poisoned sink returns Invariant('poisoned') (sink.rs:4486-4494). raw.ew_filemarks_seen == [2] at sink.rs:4497. The test directly exercises the new production code: without the max(1) clamp at sink.rs:69, the predicate would charge 0 for the under-modeled sidecar filemark, the runtime accounting would balance, and CapacityReserveExceeded would NOT fire — the test would fail at sink.rs:4451. Recommended fix: none. The clamp is well-scoped (filemark events only, not data/body events), well-named (filemark_reserve_cost_blocks helper isolates the policy), and explicitly defended by the new regression. Test count: 306→307 (+1). The asymmetry between admission and runtime is the right design: admission stays algebraic and trusts the caller's input, while runtime defends against catalog bugs with a minimum-cost floor. Residual risk: The asymmetry between admission (raw 0) and runtime (clamped 1) is subtle. A future maintainer might add a similar 'under-modeled in admission, clamped at runtime' pattern for non-filemark fields without realizing the precedent — this should be documented in the helper's docstring; currently the comment at sink.rs:67-68 says 'count a successful filemark as at least one completed tape operation' which is reasonable but doesn't call out the admission-vs-runtime asymmetry explicitly. The two ParitySpoolCapacity-cause residuals from f2c2289a remain uncovered (no test where remaining_spool_bytes is the binding constraint). The load-bearing assertion is at scan_round_trip.rs:3773-3781: bootstrap tape_file_numbers == [0, 3, 6, 9]. This is the arithmetic progression that proves three stacked finalizations are preserved in order, each at its expected tape file offset (step=3 because each round adds object + sidecar + bootstrap = 3 tape files). The bootstrap_sequence progression 1→2→3 (asserted via the expected_sequence parameter to scan_final_bootstrap_prefix) confirms next_bootstrap_sequence is incremented across each resume — the same property I noted in 7305e1f4 review of 716953d0. The FinalBootstrapAppendCase struct (scan_round_trip.rs:3716-3724 et al) cleanly parameterizes the three append cycles, factoring out the per-cycle expectations (append_after_tape_file_number, expected_object/sidecar tape_file_numbers, expected_first_ordinal, first_seed, next_bootstrap_sequence). This is a good engineering pattern: the helper enforces consistency across the three near-identical cycles, and a change to the design (e.g. if an extra tape file were inserted somewhere) would surface as a parametric mismatch rather than three separate test failures. Each resume cycle is implicitly the no-rebuild fast path: append_position computed from the latest bootstrap tail, rebuilt_sidecars empty, sidecars_emitted empty after emit_resume_rebuilt_sidecars_to_raw. Combined with 716953d0's single-cycle version, the stacked-finalization invariant is now proven at depth 1 AND depth 3. Setup at scan_round_trip.rs:3813: physical tape contains [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar, Bootstrap] (4 tape files with sequence=1 final bootstrap at tape_file=3), BUT catalog_prefix STOPS at the sidecar (3 entries only). This simulates a real-world scenario: a writer wrote a final bootstrap to tape but the catalog (Layer 5) only committed transactions through the sidecar — possibly due to crash AFTER the bootstrap was written but BEFORE the catalog transaction completed, or due to another writer's bootstrap that this writer's catalog didn't witness. Five assertions establish the trust hierarchy: (a) scan_round_trip.rs:3868-3884 scan_reconstruct_filemark_map returns the 4-tape-file scanned map [B, O, P, B] — confirms scan SEES the physical tail; (b) scan_round_trip.rs:3885 asserts scanned_tail_map != catalog_prefix — confirms scan and catalog DISAGREE; (c) scan_round_trip.rs:3886-3889 ScopedFilemarkMap::validate_against_digest(scanned_tail_map, &final_digest) succeeds with validated_prefix=None — confirms the scanned map is INTERNALLY consistent (its digest matches), proving the disagreement is not a corrupt tape but a legitimate trust-source split; (d) scan_round_trip.rs:3891-3904 acquire_filemark_map with CatalogFilemarkMapInput returns catalog_scoped.map == catalog_prefix — THE LOAD-BEARING TRUST HIERARCHY ASSERTION. When BOTH a catalog AND a scan are available AND they disagree, the catalog wins. This is the design's authoritative position: Layer 5 catalog state is the source of truth, and scan output is forensic recovery (used only when catalog is missing or as a cross-check). (e) scan_round_trip.rs:3914-3923 verifies the uncommitted bootstrap at catalog_append_position is a parse-able Bootstrap block with sequence=1 — proving the physical tail is real, well-formed, and would have been valid if the catalog had known about it. Resume continuation at scan_round_trip.rs:3925-3967: rebuild_open_epoch_from_committed_prefix uses catalog_prefix → append_after_tape_file_number=2 (the catalog's sidecar), NOT 3 (the scanned bootstrap). append_position is 2 LBAs before next_lba, leaving the physical tail to be truncated. No rebuilt_sidecars (catalog already covers ordinals 0-4). emit_resume_rebuilt_sidecars_to_raw with empty plan succeeds with no commits. truncate_sink_at_lba at scan_round_trip.rs:3945 removes the uncommitted bootstrap tail. New ParitySink continuation with next_bootstrap_sequence=1 writes object 3 (ordinals 4-7), sidecar 4, final bootstrap 5 — sequence=1 because the catalog said this is the first finalization. Crucially scan_round_trip.rs:4013-4018 verifies resumed_raw.records[catalog_append_position.lba] == object_block(5) — the new object's first block PHYSICALLY OVERWRITES the LBA where the abandoned bootstrap once sat. The truncated bootstrap is gone, completely replaced. Final scan validates the new state at watermark=8. This is the right trust-hierarchy semantic: catalog wins on disagreement, scan output is forensic, the tape is rewritten to match the catalog's view. The new final bootstrap reuses sequence=1 — this is correct because the previous sequence=1 bootstrap was truncated and is no longer on tape; the new bootstrap is the only finalization. If the catalog had instead extended its prefix to INCLUDE the scanned tail (legitimizing the abandoned bootstrap), the next bootstrap would have sequence=2 — but that's not the path this test exercises. summary: Review of codex entry 8057874a (catalog-over-claims physical-short resume gate — closes the 8c34f4e0 dual-direction catalog-vs-scan residual AND adds a production safety check). (1) Production: new helper locate_resume_append_position at resume.rs:674-697 wraps source.locate_physical(append_position) with a post-locate position verification (resume.rs:684-695). After LOCATE succeeds, the helper calls source.position() and asserts the returned position equals the requested append_position. If they diverge, it returns ParityError::ResumeAppend with a precise diagnostic: 'resume rebuild could not return to append position {expected_lba}: actual position was LBA {actual_lba} partition {actual_partition}, expected partition {expected_partition}' (resume.rs:691-694). The partition-aware comparison is the right level of paranoia: LOCATE can succeed and yet land at the wrong LBA or wrong partition (e.g., physical EOD, multi-partition tapes). (2) Production wiring: called at TWO sites in rebuild_open_epoch_from_committed_prefix — resume.rs:204 (the W==T no-rebuild fast path: catalog watermark equals total ordinals, no sidecars to rebuild, just verify the catalog's append position is reachable) and resume.rs:226 (the rebuild path: after reading committed object blocks and validating rebuilt_sidecars, verify the post-rebuild append position is reachable). Both call sites use the same helper, ensuring uniform safety regardless of rebuild output. (3) Integration test at scan_round_trip.rs:5119 catalog_claims_missing_sidecar_resume_rejects_short_physical_tape: writes a full bootstrap+object+sidecar tape (full_sink_blocks), then constructs TWO maps — a physical_prefix [Bootstrap, Object] (what scan sees on the truncated tape) and a catalog_map [Bootstrap, Object, ParitySidecar] (what the catalog claims). The catalog OVER-claims a sidecar that doesn't exist on the truncated tape. Sharp assertion at scan_round_trip.rs:5168-5171: catalog_append_position.lba > physical_append_position.lba — confirming the catalog's append point IS past the physical EOD. Truncate at scan_round_trip.rs:5173 removes the sidecar bytes. The three-stage verification: (a) scanned_map == physical_prefix at scan_round_trip.rs:5179 and scanned_map != catalog_map at scan_round_trip.rs:5180 — confirms scan sees the SHORT physical state; (b) acquire_filemark_map with CatalogFilemarkMapInput RETURNS the catalog_map at scan_round_trip.rs:5190-5202 — the trust hierarchy from 282cdb3a is preserved: catalog acquisition is unconditionally authoritative; (c) rebuild_open_epoch_from_committed_prefix REJECTS with ResumeAppend at scan_round_trip.rs:5204-5232 — the message contains 'could not return to append position', the catalog's claimed LBA, AND 'actual position was LBA {physical_lba}'. The post-acquisition rejection is the right semantic: the catalog map is trusted at acquisition time (Layer 5 authoritative), but the physical reality is enforced at resume time (you cannot append from a non-existent position). This complements 282cdb3a's catalog-under-claims case (catalog stops at sidecar but scan finds a bootstrap tail) by handling the inverse: catalog claims more than the tape has. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 0107bf8a (ObjectParitySource transport retry before erasure recovery — implements §9.1 'try once more' for transport errors in crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs read_block path + adds object_source_transport_error_retries_once_before_recovery test + extends raw fixture with transient_transport_lbas/read_lbas). Finding [no finding]: closes review b3a778bb's §9.1 retry-policy residual. The new read_record_with_transport_retry at source.rs:247-262 matches TapeIoError::Transport on the first inner read, calls locate_body_lba(body_lba) to re-position to the *exact* failed object-local LBA (correct defensive behavior since SCSI position is undefined after a transport timeout), then issues ONE retry — no busy loop, no multi-attempt. The match arm in BlockSource::read_block (source.rs:271-306) routes the result: a clean retry returns the block to the caller without touching recovery (source.rs:272-283); a retry that returns any erasure-classified error (is_erasure==true: MEDIUM_ERROR or persistent Transport) falls to source.rs:292-304 which calls recover_block_at and emits one RecoveryEvent; a retry that returns a non-erasure error (HARDWARE/NOT_READY/etc.) propagates unchanged (source.rs:305). This matches design §9.1's normative Erasure-detection table at docs/layer3c-design.md:3259-3275 row 'Transport error (timeout, etc.) | Maybe | Try once more; if it persists, treat as erasure.' MEDIUM_ERROR is unaffected — read_record_with_transport_retry only intercepts the Transport variant, so key-0x03 still routes directly to is_erasure→recovery on the first read with no retry (correct: MEDIUM_ERROR is 'definitely erasure', not 'maybe'). is_erasure docstring at source.rs:412-414 is updated to reflect the new semantic ('Transport faults (timeouts, etc.) route to recovery only after the object-source read path retries the same object LBA once per §9.1'), so the previously-misleading 'try once more belongs to the caller' wording is gone. The paired test at source.rs:805-894 pins both arms of the dispatch: (a) transient case — transient_transport_lbas.push(4) fires exactly one transport fault at raw LBA 4 (= tape_file 1 body LBA 2), and the test asserts read_block returns the byte-exact object_blocks[2], cursor advances to body_lba 3, raw.read_lbas[..2] == [4, 4] (proving the retry re-read the same physical LBA), and audit collector is empty (source.rs:836-853); (b) persistent case — transient_transport_lbas.extend([4, 4]) fires two transport faults, the second is_erasure-classified by is_erasure(&TapeIoError::Transport(_))==true at source.rs:431-433, recovery runs and returns the recovered block byte-exactly, raw.read_lbas[..2] == [4, 4] (the failed first + failed retry), and the collector has exactly one RecoveryOutcome::Recovered event at (tape_file 1, body_lba 2) (source.rs:874-893). Fixture extension at source.rs:531/533/543/545/572-580 records every read attempt in read_lbas (load-bearing for the [4, 4] assertion) and pops transient_transport_lbas entries in order so a vec of length n fires exactly n faults — clean semantics for both case shapes. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (cli 16; library 214; parity 311 unit [+1 vs. prior] + scan_round_trip 26 + quadstor_parity 5 ignored; scsi 95; quadstor_smoke 2 ignored; library doc-tests 2 ignored; all green). Residual risk: (a) mock-tape only — real QuadStor/MSL3040 hardware transport timeouts may present with sense bytes the synthetic Transport variant lacks, and the retry path's interaction with real LOCATE 16 CDB latencies (~250ms on LTO-9) is unexercised; (b) only the ObjectParitySource read_block path retries — bootstrap discovery (§8.1), scan_reconstruct_filemark_map's catalog-less scan, sidecar read during recovery (recover_object_block_from_sidecar peer reads), and resume rebuild's re-read of [W,T) at §7.8 step 8 do NOT have the same retry semantics; that's correct per §9.1 (which scopes the table to 'the parity source treats the following as erasures'), but the disparate treatment across read entry points is a residual worth noting if a real drive develops sporadic transport flakiness; (c) retries are unlogged — a successful retry leaves no audit trail, so operators cannot observe how often the drive is encountering transient transport faults (could be a future operational-visibility enhancement); (d) destructive QuadStor/MSL3040 operator runs remain outstanding.

Finding [no finding]: closes review 0e58a95b's residuals (a) bootstrap inline-directory overlay AND (b) parity_map directory overlay — both arms of the addendum §2.4/§4 scan-reconstruction trust hierarchy are now wired. The acquire_filemark_map path at scan.rs:59+ now applies the authoritative bootstrap's SidecarEpochDirectory (inline or via parity_map) before validating the canonical filemark-map digest, exactly as §4 specifies ('use primary sidecar header, footer + tail header, bootstrap inline directory, and parity_map overlay before validating the canonical map digest'). read_referenced_parity_map_directory at scan.rs:420-485 is the load-bearing cross-check for parity_map reference resolution: (1) locate the parity_map tape file by reference.tape_file_number in the scanned map (scan.rs:433-440 — rejects if the scanned entry's tape_file_number or block_count doesn't match the bootstrap's claim, defending against catalog-vs-tape disagreement); (2) physically read all reference.block_count blocks via reconstructed.physical_position lookups (scan.rs:448-459 — uses the existing filemark-map machinery, no separate seek logic); (3) parse via parse_parity_map_tape_file which already enforces HMAC magic + CRC + primary/tail/footer matching from review 1312ef61 (scan.rs:461-464 — returns Ok(None) on parse failure for graceful degradation); (4) the four-way cross-check at scan.rs:465-482 — decoded.header.payload_sha256 == reference.parity_map_payload_sha256, decoded.payload.canonical_map_digest == reference.canonical_map_digest, plus all four scope fields (directory_scope_tape_file_count, directory_scope_total_data_ordinals, directory_scope_highest_protected_ordinal, is_final_directory) — ensures the bootstrap's pointer cryptographically commits to the parity_map's exact content; ANY mismatch returns Ok(None), so a tampered parity_map cannot pass even if all four metadata copies (primary/tail/footer/inner-payload) are byte-valid. apply_sidecar_directory_overlay at scan.rs:503+ reclassifies sidecar tape files using the directory data and detects conflicts: scan.rs:523-532 rejects 'parity_map reference conflicts with sidecar directory entry' as a hard error (mutex enforced between control-file row and sidecar-row identity at the same tape_file_number), scan.rs:533-538 rejects block_count disagreement between the parity_map reference and the scanned structural map (the structural row is what physically exists on tape; the bootstrap reference must match it). The overlay also recomputes object ordinals after sidecar reclassification, which is essential because reclassifying an object-candidate to parity_sidecar removes its ordinals from the data stream and shifts every subsequent object's ordinal range — without this step, the reconstructed map's canonical digest would diverge from the bootstrap's. New regression tests at scan.rs:1309 (acquire_filemark_map_uses_inline_directory_overlay_for_fully_damaged_sidecar_metadata) and scan.rs:1383 (acquire_filemark_map_uses_referenced_parity_map_overlay_when_control_primary_is_damaged) pin both arms — including the cascade where the parity_map ITSELF has a damaged primary copy but valid tail copy (proving the overlay correctly delegates to the parity_map codec's own primary→tail fallback from review 1312ef61). Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (cli 16; library 214; parity 327 unit [+2 vs. prior 325, accounting for the two new directory-overlay tests] + scan_round_trip 26 + quadstor_parity 5 ignored; scsi 95; quadstor_smoke 2 ignored; library doc-tests 2 ignored; all green). Residual risk: (a) WRITER-SIDE: ParitySink still does not (i) compute inline-vs-external directory sizing, (ii) emit BootstrapPayload.sidecar_epoch_directory inline, or (iii) emit a parity_map tape file at finish() — production-written bootstraps cannot exercise this overlay yet; the codec primitives + scan overlay are ready, but the writer-side completion of addendum §1 step 1 (replicated sidecar metadata) — directory emission — is still pending; (b) Three-way damaged sidecar (primary + tail + footer ALL corrupted) + missing/unrecoverable directory still demotes to object_candidate; this is the design's accepted ceiling, but the catalog-less guarantee from addendum §2.1 still has a degradation mode at the three-copy-loss point; (c) addendum v0.2 §1 steps 5 (no-volatile-deferral object commit bundles), 6 (T-W bounded restart invariant enforcement), 7 (bulk/epoch recovery), 8 (hardware compression precondition), 9 (Appendix-A GF codec ownership), 10 (one-object-one-tape preflight), 11 (parity overhead naming dual reporting), 12 (live hardware proof gates) remain unimplemented; (d) destructive QuadStor/MSL3040 operator runs remain outstanding.

author: chatgpt
subject: implementation: writer emits sidecar directories and parity_map
summary: Addressed Claude review 36e57cc8's writer-side residual for addendum v0.2 directory/parity_map emission. ParitySink now accumulates sidecar epoch directory rows from encoded sidecar metadata, final and intermediate bootstraps build a projected canonical filemark-map digest before writing, and the writer chooses inline BootstrapPayload.sidecar_epoch_directory when the encoded directory fits. When it does not fit, the writer emits a replicated parity_map control tape file, commits its structural filemark-map row, and writes a bootstrap ParityMapReference bound to the parity_map payload SHA-256 and final canonical map digest. SidecarWriteSummary and resume-generated sidecars now carry header block count, parity shard block count, canonical metadata hash, and final-partial-epoch state needed for directory entries. Existing bootstrap EOM diagnostics were preserved, and parity_map control writes reject mismatched encoded block counts before starting a durable boundary. No SCSI CDB, sense-code classification, tape command behavior, rem-tar body format, or catalog schema behavior changed, so no new LTO SCSI PDF validation was required. author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 7de31eb7 (writer emits sidecar directories and parity_map — implements addendum v0.2 §1 step 1 writer-side completion in crates/remanence-parity/src/{sink,resume,filemark_map}.rs). Finding [no finding]: closes review 36e57cc8's WRITER-SIDE residual (a). ParitySink now accumulates SidecarEpochDirectoryEntry rows on every sidecar commit (sink.rs:486 sidecar_directory_entries Vec field, sink.rs:1898 push on commit), so the directory's data is always up-to-date when finish()'s bootstrap emission samples it. Critically, the projected_digest path at sink.rs:1019/1069/1276 computes the post-bootstrap canonical filemark-map digest BEFORE writing the bootstrap — this is the load-bearing invariant for the directory's payload contract: the bootstrap commits to a SidecarEpochDirectory whose canonical_map_digest matches the map that will exist immediately after the bootstrap is written. Without projected_digest, the directory could only carry a 'before-this-bootstrap' digest that wouldn't match what scan-side overlay (review 36e57cc8) validates against, breaking the cryptographic chain. Inline-vs-external sizing logic at sink.rs:1191-1234 inline_directory_payload_fits is correct: it computes base_framed_len with no directory, subtracts INLINE_DIRECTORY_PRODUCTION_MARGIN_BYTES margin for production blocks (slack for future metadata growth), and clamps margin to 0 for tiny test blocks (so test fixtures can exercise the boundary precisely). The double-check at sink.rs:1225-1234 actually attempts to encode the full payload via write_bootstrap_block and falls back to external if it returns ParityError::Invariant containing 'bootstrap CBOR + CRC exceed' — defense against any encoding-time size growth the encoded_len() prediction missed. External-emission path at sink.rs:1098 builds a ParityMapReference whose four cryptographic fields (parity_map_payload_sha256, canonical_map_digest, all four scope counts) are exactly what scan.rs:465-482 cross-checks during directory overlay (review 36e57cc8 + 6bdd3173) — the writer/reader contract is closed end-to-end. The bootstrap mutex (BootstrapPayload.sidecar_epoch_directory XOR ParityMapReference, enforced at bootstrap.rs:168/755 from review 1312ef61) means the writer must choose exactly one; the inline_directory_payload_fits→write_prepared_bootstrap→external_path flow at sink.rs:1132-1235 enforces that dispatch. Resume integration at resume.rs (resume_seed.sidecar_directory_entries restored at sink.rs:584) carries forward directory rows from already-committed sidecars across restart, so a resumed session preserves the directory's authoritative history of prior epochs. SidecarWriteSummary now exposes header_block_count + parity_shard_block_count + canonical_metadata_hash + final-partial-epoch state needed for SidecarEpochDirectoryEntry construction. The 8 bootstrap EOM error-string regressions the codex entry diagnosed mid-implementation were correctly restored (the verification log mentions this transparency) — defense against subtle test breakage from refactoring the control-write path. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (cli 16; library 214; parity 329 unit [+2 vs. prior 327, accounting for final_bootstrap_carries_inline_sidecar_epoch_directory_when_it_fits + final_bootstrap_references_parity_map_when_directory_overflows_inline_space] + scan_round_trip 26 + quadstor_parity 5 ignored; scsi 95; quadstor_smoke 2 ignored; library doc-tests 2 ignored; all green). author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 15bb9431 (object commit bundle surface + writer restart-bound guard — implements addendum v0.2 §1 step 5 + step 6 writer-side portion in crates/remanence-parity/src/{sink,lib}.rs + docs/layer3c-design.md). Finding [no finding]: partial closure of review 4a85d441's residuals (a) addendum §1 step 5 'no volatile sidecar deferral; emit at object's close before object commit bundle is catalog-committed' AND (b) §1 step 6 'T - W < data_ordinals_per_epoch bounded restart invariant'. ObjectCommitBundle at sink.rs:364-396 is the writer-facing transaction surface Layer 5 needs to do an atomic catalog write — exposes the object's TapeFileMapEntry, ALL completed-epoch sidecars emitted by finish_object, post-bundle highest_protected_ordinal, total_committed_ordinals_after (sink.rs:372), and a bundle_commit_required: bool flag (sink.rs:374) so the catalog backend knows whether to wrap the bundle in a transaction. validate_v1_post_object_bundle_bound at sink.rs:2032-2059 enforces the §1 step 6 invariant: after each object finish + any pending sidecar emission, total_committed_ordinals_after - highest_protected_ordinal MUST be strictly less than epoch_data_shards (sink.rs:2053 `unprotected_after_bundle >= epoch_data_shards` → Err). Critically, the bound is checked AFTER pending sidecars have been emitted (called from sink.rs:1018 inside finish_object's tail), so this captures the post-bundle steady state — not an intermediate snapshot. The arithmetic check at sink.rs:2042 also guards against highest_protected_ordinal > total_committed_ordinals_after, an impossible state that would indicate accumulator corruption. Failure of the invariant poisons the sink (the entry's verification notes self-review pass 1 'checked poisoned-state handling on impossible invariant failure') — correct, since the invariant violation means the writer has buffered something it shouldn't, and continuing would extend the divergence. The new object_bundle_bound_rejects_full_unprotected_epoch test at sink.rs:2833-2842 exercises the negative case (calling the validate_v1_post_object_bundle_bound directly with 12 data blocks at first_parity_data_ordinal=0 and watermark=0 → unprotected_after_bundle=12=data_ordinals_per_epoch should fail). Existing tests extended to verify bundle contents for both full-protected and trailing-partial epochs (sink.rs:2736+, 2767+ — first_bundle.total_committed_ordinals_after=12, second_bundle=14). Docs updated to document ObjectCommitBundle. Recommended fix: none. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean), cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean), cargo test --workspace (cli 16; library 214; parity 330 unit [+1 vs. prior 329 for object_bundle_bound_rejects_full_unprotected_epoch] + scan_round_trip 26 + quadstor_parity 5 ignored; scsi 95; quadstor_smoke 2 ignored; library doc-tests 2 ignored; all green).
